critique.summary: The article reports that the US government and TotalEnergies have reached an agreement worth nearly $1 billion to end the French company's offshore wind projects in the US and redirect the funds towards fossil fuel production, particularly natural gas. The deal was announced by US Interior Secretary Doug Burgum and TotalEnergies CEO Patrick Pouyanné at the CERAWeek conference in Houston on March 23, 2026.
mainPoints: TotalEnergies agreed to halt its offshore wind farm projects in the US, including leases off North Carolina and New York coasts.
The nearly $1 billion investment originally intended for offshore wind will be redirected to US natural gas projects, such as the Rio Grande LNG plant.
The deal was announced by US Interior Secretary Doug Burgum and TotalEnergies CEO Patrick Pouyanné at the CERAWeek conference.
TotalEnergies had put its offshore wind projects on hold due to difficulties obtaining federal licenses under the Trump administration.
The Trump administration reversed many of Biden's climate policies and had put several wind projects on hold citing national security concerns, though courts later allowed work to resume.
TotalEnergies CEO stated that offshore wind is not the most affordable electricity source in the US compared to natural gas.
TotalEnergies also signed a letter of intent for long-term LNG offtake from the Alaska LNG project.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article align with publicly available information about US energy policy shifts under the Trump administration and TotalEnergies' strategic decisions. The reported $1 billion deal and the redirection of funds from offshore wind to natural gas projects are plausible given the political context and statements from involved officials. However, the article relies heavily on statements from the US Interior Secretary and TotalEnergies CEO without citing independent sources or providing multiple perspectives, which limits verification. The timeline and details about project holds and policy reversals correspond with known events, but the article does not provide corroboration from other independent or governmental sources.
considerations: The article does not provide perspectives from environmental groups or renewable energy advocates who might contest the deal.
It lacks detailed analysis of the environmental impact of shifting investments from offshore wind to fossil fuels.
The article does not clarify the current status of offshore wind projects under the Biden administration beyond the Trump-era policy reversals.
There is no discussion of the broader US energy market or how this deal fits into long-term climate goals.
The article does not address potential economic or job impacts of ending offshore wind projects.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on a nearly $1 billion deal between the US government and TotalEnergies to cancel offshore wind projects, with debate over whether this constitutes a government payment or a lease deposit refund. Commenters express a mix of skepticism, concern about the US energy policy direction, and reflections on renewable energy economics and political motivations.

comments.author: Ajedi32
comments.text:
HN title (currently reads "US govt pays TotalEnergies nearly $1B to stop US offshore wind projects") is editorialized and it's unclear to me whether it's accurate. The article says:

> We're partnering with TotalEnergies to unleash nearly $1 billion that was tied up in a lease deposit that was directed towards the prior administration's subsidies

What's the deal with this lease deposit and how does "freeing it up" equate to the US govt "paying" TotalEnergies that amount?

Is this a situation where TotalEnergies put down a 1B deposit to lease the seashore from the government and the government is now canceling that agreement and giving them their money back? How does it relate to "subsidies"?
comments.analysis: This comment carefully questions the framing of the deal and distinguishes between a refund and a payment, highlighting important nuances in interpreting the article.

comments.author: pptr
comments.text:
This really seems like a nothing burger.

The $1B were a refund. Net exchange ~$0.

Building out fossile fuel production shifts oil revenue from various dictatorships around the world to the US in this case. That's a good thing. I wish we in Europe produced more gas ourselves instead of being highly dependent on other countries.

This does not mean higher gas demand, which is what matters for CO2 reduction.
comments.analysis: Provides a balanced perspective emphasizing the financial neutrality of the deal and geopolitical implications, aligning with known facts about energy markets.

comments.author: andyjohnson0
comments.text:
> "TotalEnergies CEO Pouyanné said offshore wind was 'not the most affordable way to produce electricity' in the US, which he identified as being natural gas-fired power plants. [...] So it was a win-win dialog," he said."

Pouyanné is only 62 years old. If, as I hope, there are criminal trials in the future for those responsible for recklessly endangering life on this planet, then I hope that he is still alive and that statements like this form part of the prosecution. Unfortunately Trump will almost certainly be long dead by then.
comments.analysis: Connects the CEO's statement to broader environmental concerns and accountability, though the mention of criminal trials is speculative.

comments.author: jmclnx
comments.text:
Sorry, I do not know how else to say this:

Well hopefully when Trump is gone NY remembers this and tells Pouyanné to screw when they put out bids to restart the project.
comments.analysis: Expresses a political opinion about future energy policy decisions, reflecting common sentiments but speculative about outcomes.

comments.author: BigTTYGothGF
comments.text: I'm reminded of Reagan taking down the White House solar panels.
comments.analysis: Draws a historical parallel to previous US government actions on renewable energy, providing context though not directly analyzing the current deal.

comments.author: raziel2701
comments.text: We keep going down the wrong direction. We need more energy self-sufficiency. More investment in solar and wind.
comments.analysis: Expresses a normative viewpoint advocating for renewables, reflecting a common environmental stance but not directly analyzing the deal.

comments.author: sameergh
comments.text: If this is accurate the US is making itself look unreliable for major energy investment
comments.analysis: Raises a valid concern about the impact of the deal on US energy investment reputation, though it is a speculative consequence.

comments.author: harmmonica
comments.text:
I know this US government is fully-committed to fossil fuels and about as rabidly anti-renewables as can be, but I'm still shocked to see things like this. And I'm fully aware of Trump's Scotland experience and how that contributed or directly led to this, but, still, shocked. And then I'm also shocked because I know that at least half, if not a good bit more, of US citizens are in agreement with this strategy. Not sure how I can still be shocked but here I am.

And I say that not as some rabid renewables person. Just the insane binary thinking, regardless of the dollars and cronyism at work. There's zero room for nuance, which I guess is my biggest complaint about the world at large.

Aside: people who think climate change will be the death of us all, and sooner than later, I get it, and I fully appreciate you pushing for a cleaner and more livable world. At this point I'm just going to sit in the corner and hope you, and China, figure it out and then it spreads quickly to the rest of the world, which I think at this point is pretty much a foregone conclusion barring a nuclear war (will refrain from commenting about how the likelihood of that has ticked up the past couple of weeks in an area teeming with (sarcastically shocked this time!) fossil fuels).
comments.analysis: Provides a nuanced reflection on political and social attitudes toward renewables, though largely opinion-based.

comments.author: angelgonzales
comments.text:
This seems like a good thing considering the “TotalEnergies CEO Pouyanné said offshore wind was "not the most affordable way to produce electricity" in the US, which he identified as being natural gas-fired power plants.”

Not sure why we’re building offshore wind plants when land based gas plants provide cheaper energy. We need to be reducing the cost of living for working people and not raising it. Our goal should be to reduce people’s cost of living and we should align our actions towards those goals.

Most people are cost sensitive!
comments.analysis: Highlights economic considerations in energy choices, emphasizing cost sensitivity which is a critical factor in energy policy.

comments.author: mikkupikku
comments.text: If the government would like to pay me to also not build wind turbines, hit me up.  I mean, I wasn't going to build any in the first place, but I think this makes me qualified to continue not building any.
comments.analysis: A sarcastic comment mocking the deal; adds humor but little substantive analysis.
